school: A wealthy and respected Meccan merchant, she was the first wife of the Prophet Muhammad and the first person to accept Islam. She employed Muhammad as a trade agent before proposing marriage, and their union lasted twenty-five years until her death in 619 CE. Her emotional, spiritual, and financial support was crucial during the earliest and most difficult years of the Prophet's mission, and she is revered in Islamic tradition as one of the greatest women in history.
parents: Khawaylid bin Asad / Fatima bint Za'idah of B.'Amir b. Lu'ayy
children: Hind bin Abi Halah , Halah bin Abi Halah , al-Harith bin Abi Halah , Tahir bin Abi Halah , Hindah, al-Qasim bin Muhammad , Zaynab bint Muhammad , Ruqayyah bint Muhammad , Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad , Fatima bint Muhammad , 'Abdullah bin Muhammad
spouses: Abu Halah-Malik bin al-Nabash al-Tamimi, Atique bin Aith, Prophet Muhammad(saw)

school: Companion of the Prophet — undefeated in 100+ battles; conquered Iraq, Syria and Persia; never lost a battle in his life; the Prophet personally named him 'Sword of God'
parents: al-Walid bin Mughairah Makhzumi / Lubaba(Sughra) bint al-Harith
children: Suliaman bin Khalid bin al-Walid , Sara bint Khalid, al-Muhajir bin Khalid bin al-Walid , 'Abdur Rahman bin Khalid bin al-Walid , 'Abdullah bin Khalid bin al-Walid
spouses: Kabsha bint Hudhah al-'Adhriyya, Asma bint Anas al-Khatha'miyya
quotes: There is no night during which a bride is prepared for her groom that is dearer to me than a night during which I prepare for battle at dawn.
I have fought in so many battles seeking martyrdom that there is no span of my body left without a scar — yet here I am, dying in my bed like an old camel.

school: A prominent Companion of the Prophet and one of the most skilled military and political leaders of early Islam, he led the Muslim conquest of Egypt in 639-642 CE. He served as the first Muslim governor of Egypt and founded the garrison city of Fustat, which later became the nucleus of Cairo. Known for his diplomatic acumen and strategic brilliance, he played a significant role in the events of the First Fitna and served Mu'awiya I as governor of Egypt until his death.
parents: As-Samit b. Qays b. Asram / Qurratu'l-'Ayn bint 'Ubada bin Nadla
children: al-Walid bin 'Ubada bin As-Samit , Muhammad bin al-Walid bin ''Aabadh
spouses: Jamila bint Abi Sa'sa'a , Umm Haram bint Milhan
quotes: I have never seen anyone more shrewd in governance than Muawiyah, nor anyone more devoted in prayer than Abu Bakr.
The world is sweet and green, and God has made you stewards in it to see how you act.

school: Elder grandson of Prophet Muhammad (624–670) and fifth caliph of Islam. He abdicated the caliphate in favour of Muawiyah to prevent further bloodshed among Muslims, fulfilling the Prophet’s prediction that ‘this son of mine is a master, and God will make peace between two great groups of Muslims through him.’ Revered by both Sunni and Shia traditions.
parents: Ali ibn Abi Talib / Fatima bint Muhammad
children: al-Hasan bin al-Hasan bin 'Ali , Qasim, Fatimah, Zayd bin al-Hasan bin 'Ali , 'Abdullah, Talha, Umm al-Hasan (Maymunah), Umm al-Husan

school: Younger grandson of Prophet Muhammad (626–680) who refused to pledge allegiance to Yazid ibn Muawiyah and was martyred at the Battle of Karbala with 72 companions. His sacrifice became the defining event of Shia Islam and a universal symbol of resistance against tyranny. The annual commemoration of Ashura is observed by Muslims worldwide.
parents: Ali ibn Abi Talib / Fatima bint Muhammad
children: 'Ali bin al-Husain bin 'Ali , 'Ali al-Akbar, 'Ali al-Asghar, Sakina bint al-Husain bin 'Ali , Fatima bint al-Husain bin 'Ali
spouses: Shahrbanu bint Yazdgerd III, Umm Rubab, Umm Layla, Umm Is'haq bint Talha

school: Pioneering female mystic of Basra (c. 717–801) who transformed Islamic spirituality by introducing the concept of selfless divine love — worshipping God neither out of fear of Hell nor hope of Paradise, but purely for His sake. Born into slavery and freed for her piety, she rejected all offers of marriage, declaring her heart belonged only to God.
quotes: O God, if I worship You in fear of Hell, burn me in Hell. If I worship You in hope of Paradise, exclude me. But if I worship You for Your own sake, do not withhold Your everlasting beauty.

O Allah, if I worship You for fear of Hell, burn me in Hell; if I worship You in hope of Paradise, exclude me from Paradise. But if I worship You for Your own sake, do not withhold from me Your Eternal Beauty.
I have made You the companion of my heart — my body is present for those who seek my company, but my Beloved is the companion of my soul.
My Lord, the stars are shining and the eyes of men are closed — kings have shut their doors. Every lover is alone with his beloved. And here I am alone with You.
I carry a torch in one hand and a bucket of water in the other — with these things I will set fire to Paradise and put out the flames of Hell.

O God, if I worship You out of fear of Hell, burn me in Hell. If I worship You out of desire for Paradise, exclude me from it. But if I worship You for Your own sake, do not withhold Your eternal beauty from me.
I carry a torch in one hand and water in the other — so that I may set fire to Paradise and extinguish Hell, and the people will worship God truly, not from fear of punishment or desire for reward.
My hope is for union with You — that is the greatest gift I seek.

school: Kufan Ascetic School — Student of Abu Hanifa who abandoned jurisprudence for complete spiritual retreat
quotes: Reduce your connection to this world, for the heart cannot be with God and with the world at the same time.
The wise man is not one who can distinguish good from evil — he is one who chooses the better of two goods.
I looked at my sins and wept; I looked at the mercy of God and hoped.

school: Basran ascetic and mystic (d. 793) who founded the first known Sufi ribat (retreat house) at Abbadan on the Persian Gulf. A student of Hasan al-Basri, he organised communal spiritual retreats that became the model for later Sufi lodges across the Islamic world. He is a crucial link between the Basran ascetic tradition and organised Sufism.

Syrian mystic and ascetic (c. 755–830) who was among the earliest Sufis to articulate the concept of spiritual knowledge (ma’rifa) as distinct from scholarly knowledge. Based near Damascus, he taught that true knowledge comes through purification of the heart. He deeply influenced the later Khorasani and Baghdadi Sufi schools.
quotes: Every moment you spend without the remembrance of God is a loss for which there is no compensation.
Whoever truly fears God, his heart becomes tender and his tongue becomes truthful.
I have not heard anything more terrifying than the words: We have tried them as We tried the people of the garden.

school: Prince of Balkh (c. 718–782) who renounced his kingdom for the ascetic life — the Islamic parallel to the Buddha’s renunciation. According to tradition, a divine voice told him while hunting that he was not created for such pursuits. He became a wandering ascetic, working as a harvester and night-watchman, and is revered as a model of worldly renunciation.
quotes: I learned true reliance on God from a monk in the desert who had nothing — and smiled.
Whoever is at peace with himself is at peace with God.
I learned true reliance on God from a monk in the desert who had nothing — and smiled.
Whoever is at peace with himself is at peace with God.

school: Former highway robber turned ascetic saint (c. 725–803) who repented after hearing a Quranic recitation while planning a robbery. He settled in Mecca, where he became one of the most revered early Sufis. Both Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal and al-Shafi’i sought his company. He famously said that seeing a scholar love the world disgusted him.
quotes: Abandon the love of leadership, and you will become a leader.
God created this world as a prison for the believer and a paradise for the disbeliever — so what kind of paradise is it that you are weeping over?
If the people praise you for what you know is not in you, fear that they will blame you for what is.
Whoever does not know the value of what he possesses will lose it without grief.
The generous soul gives before it is asked — it has already sensed the need.
Do not expose your need to one who has no capacity for generosity.

school: Baghdadi saint (d. 815) of probable Mandaean or Christian origin who converted to Islam and became one of the most revered early Sufis. He was the teacher of Sari al-Saqati, who in turn taught Junayd — making Ma’ruf a crucial link in the chain that would define classical Sufism. His tomb in the Karkh district of Baghdad remains a pilgrimage site.
quotes: The love of God cannot enter a heart in which love of this world resides.
Seize goodness for yourself before it is seized from you.
I am amazed at one who knows that death is true, how he can still laugh.

school: Prophetic Household (Ahl al-Bayt) — eighth Imam of Twelver Shia; designated heir of Caliph al-Mamun; buried in Mashhad
quotes: Faith is knowing with the heart, confessing with the tongue, and acting with the limbs.
The best of people is he who is most beneficial to people.
Whoever does not thank human beings has not thanked God.
Faith is knowing with the heart, confessing with the tongue, and acting with the limbs.
The best of people is he who is most beneficial to people.
Whoever does not thank human beings has not thanked God.

school: Baghdadi ascetic and mystic (c. 767–841) known as ‘al-Hafi’ (the Barefoot) because he refused to wear shoes, saying he would not tread on God’s earth with arrogance. A former dissolute who repented after finding a piece of paper bearing the name of God, he became one of the most revered figures of early Muslim piety.
quotes: The first degree of scrupulousness is to leave everything doubtful; the highest degree is to leave everything that is not for God.
I have never seen anyone who truly knew God and yet was not humble before His creation.
Do not be deceived by the sweetness of worship — ask whether it is accepted.

school: Baghdadi theologian and mystic (781–857) who founded the discipline of systematic self-examination (muhasaba) — a rigorous accounting of the soul’s states and intentions. His Kitab al-Ri’aya (Book of Observance) profoundly influenced al-Ghazali’s later work. He combined Mu’tazili-trained rationalism with deep Sufi introspection.
quotes: Examine your soul before you are examined.
Examine your own soul before you examine others — for the one who does not know himself cannot guide anyone else.
The sign of the intelligent man is that he holds his own soul to account more than he holds others.

school: Khorasan School — one of very few women acknowledged by Bayazid Bastami as his spiritual superior; her teachings were transmitted orally through the Sufi lineages of Nishapur

Egyptian mystic and alchemist (c. 796–859) considered the first to formulate the Sufi concept of ma’rifa (gnosis) — direct experiential knowledge of God as distinct from scholarly learning. He was also interested in ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics and alchemy. Accused of heresy, he was summoned to Baghdad but acquitted by the caliph.
quotes: I never knew God as He should be known until I looked at the face of a sleeping man and was awed by the power that could stop him or wake him.

Three signs mark the knower of God: his light does not extinguish his reverence, he accepts no inner knowledge that contradicts the outer law, and worldly abundance does not tempt him to transgression.
The sign of love for God is following the Beloved of God in his character, his commands, his actions, and his Sunnah.
The gnostic grows more humble every hour, because every hour draws him nearer to God.
Three things are signs of the knower: his light does not extinguish his reverence, his knowledge does not erase his scrupulousness, and the abundance of gifts does not make him greedy.

school: Baghdadi mystic (c. 765–867) regarded as the first Sufi to systematically discuss the spiritual states (ahwal) and stations (maqamat). He was the uncle and teacher of Junayd of Baghdad, thereby linking the earliest Baghdadi ascetics to the classical Sufi tradition. He spent decades in devoted worship and was famous for his extreme scrupulousness.
quotes: Whoever claims to have reached God through ease — his claim is false.
The heart that is sound fears every passing moment that it might have displeased God.
Three things are the mark of the intelligent: to control the tongue, to reflect before speaking, and to weigh every action.

school: Persian mystic (c. 804–874) who pioneered the ‘intoxicated’ (sukri) school of Sufism, speaking of annihilation of the self (fana) in God. His ecstatic utterances (shathiyat), such as ‘Glory be to me! How great is my majesty!’ shocked the orthodox but became foundational to later mystical theology. He never wrote; his sayings were preserved by disciples.
quotes: Thirty years the divine reality was my mirror — now I am the mirror of the divine reality.
I went from God to God, until they cried from me in me: O thou I.
Glory be to me — how great is my majesty! said the tongue of the Truth through me.
I looked — and found that lover and beloved and love are one.

books.title: Memorial of the Saints (Attar) - chapter on Bayazid

school: Prophetic Household (Ahl al-Bayt) — ninth Imam of Twelver Shia; became Imam at age 9; renowned for answers to theological questions
quotes: Trust in God is the strongest fortress.
Whoever controls his anger, God will cover his faults.
The generous man is near to God, near to paradise, near to people — the miser is far from all of these.

school: Khorasani scholar and mystic (c. 820–910) who wrote the first systematic theory of sainthood (walaya) in Islam. His Seal of the Saints influenced Ibn Arabi centuries later. Not to be confused with the hadith collector al-Tirmidhi, he combined hadith scholarship with deep mystical insight.
quotes: The soul that submits to God has no need to submit to other than God.
Prophethood is the gate; sainthood is the city behind it.
Knowledge of the heart is the fruit of renunciation.

school: The ‘Master of the Masters’ of Sufism (c. 830–910) who established the ‘sober’ school of Baghdad mysticism, emphasising that the mystic must return to everyday life after the experience of union with God. Nearly every major Sufi order traces its spiritual chain through Junayd. He insisted that Sufism must remain grounded in the Quran and Sunnah.
quotes: Sufism is that God makes you die to yourself and live in Him.
Our knowledge is bound by the Book and the Sunnah. Whoever has not memorised the Quran and studied hadith is not fit to speak on this path.
Sufism is that Allah makes you die to yourself and live through Him.
The way to God is not long — it is as close as your own heart. The problem is that you are not in your heart.
All paths are closed except the one who follows in the footsteps of the Prophet.
The highest degree of sincerity is that you do not seek any witness to your deeds — not even yourself.
If a man reaches God, he returns to the people — carrying God with him.
Tawhid is that you single out the Eternal from the temporal.
The highest station of the mystic is the return — after the ascent, back to full engagement with creation.
I heard from God, by God, for God, and to God.

school: Baghdadi mystic (c. 840–907) who was a close companion of Junayd and al-Hallaj. When Sufis were arrested in Baghdad and sentenced to death, al-Nuri stepped forward and offered to be executed first, saying he wished to give his companions one more moment of life. The judge was so moved that he released them all.

Mansur al-Hallaj (858–922), Persian mystic executed in Baghdad for declaring ‘Ana al-Haqq’ (I am the Truth). His public proclamation of mystical union with God scandalized the authorities. After years of imprisonment, he was tortured and crucified. His martyrdom became the most debated event in Sufi history — condemned by some scholars, revered by others as the supreme testimony of divine love.

books.title: The Mystics of Islam (chapter on al-Hallaj)

quotes: I am the Truth.
Kill me, O my trusted friends — for in my being killed is my life.
I am neither East nor West, neither land nor sea. I am neither natural nor spiritual. I am neither fire, earth, water nor air.

school: Baghdad School — direct student of Junayd and intimate companion of al-Hallaj; executed for refusing to denounce Hallaj, completing the pair of Baghdad's greatest martyrs

Baghdadi Sufi and companion of al-Hallaj (861–946) who served as a government official before abandoning worldly life for the mystical path. Known for his ecstatic utterances and unconventional behaviour, he was repeatedly confined for madness. He is one of the most frequently quoted Sufis in classical mystical literature.

Abu Abd Allah Muhammad ibn Khafif al-Shirazi (882–982) was one of the most revered Sufi masters of the 10th century, known as al-Shaykh al-Kabir (The Great Shaykh). Based in Shiraz, he was a link between the Iraqi and Khorasani schools of Sufism, having studied under masters from both traditions including al-Hallaj's circle. He emphasised the strict observance of the Sharia alongside the Sufi path, making Sufism respectable to the jurists. His travels took him across the Islamic world — from India to Egypt — and his hospitality and generosity became legendary. He lived to the extraordinary age of one hundred, serving as the spiritual anchor of Shiraz for decades. His influence shaped the later Sufi traditions of Iran, and his tomb in Shiraz was venerated for centuries.

Khorasani Sufi scholar (d. 988) whose Kitab al-Luma’ (Book of Flashes) was the first comprehensive encyclopedia of Sufism. It catalogued the states, stations, terminology, and practices of the mystics with scholarly precision, becoming the foundational reference that al-Qushayri, al-Hujwiri, and later Sufi writers built upon.
quotes: The speech of the mystics is the subtlest and most difficult of all speech.

school: Baghdad School — wrote the foundational manual of Islamic spiritual practice that Al-Ghazali used as a model for the Ihya
quotes: The heart is the seat of knowledge, and the tongue is its interpreter.
The heart is the throne of God on earth — do not allow anything other than God to sit upon it.
Eat little, sleep little, speak little — and you will find the door of the heart opening.

school: Khorasani Sufi scholar (d. 988) who wrote the Kitab al-Luma’ (Book of Flashes), the first comprehensive encyclopedia of Sufism. The work systematically categorised Sufi terminology, practices, states, and stations, providing a scholarly framework that later writers like al-Qushayri and al-Hujwiri built upon.

Khorasan School — the great compiler of early Sufi biography and Quranic commentary; his Tabaqat al-Sufiyya was translated into Persian by Khwaja Abdullah Ansari and became the main source for all later Sufi biographical dictionaries
quotes: The Sufi is the son of the present moment.
The Sufi is not the one who wears the cloak — the Sufi is the one whose heart is free of everything other than God.
The beginning of the path is sincerity; the middle is sincerity; the end is sincerity.

school: Hanafi scholar and Sufi from Bukhara (d. 990 or 995) who wrote al-Ta’arruf, one of the earliest systematic introductions to Sufism. His work aimed to reconcile Sufi practice with orthodox Sunni theology, demonstrating that the great Sufis were faithful Muslims. The book became a key reference for understanding classical Sufi doctrine.
quotes: The Sufi sees nothing but God in all things.

school: Khorasani scholar and mystic (986–1072) whose Risala (Epistle on Sufism) became the most widely studied Sufi textbook in the Islamic world. He combined rigorous Ash’ari theology with Sufi practice, insisting that authentic mysticism must be rooted in orthodox belief. He studied under al-Daqqaq and was a contemporary of al-Hujwiri.
quotes: The beginning of the spiritual path is knowledge, its middle is action, and its end is a gift from God.
Contentment is not that you possess nothing — it is that nothing possesses you.
The Sufi path begins with sincerity and ends with sincerity — everything in between is also sincerity.
The mark of success on this path is that you become less impressed with yourself each day.
Whoever does not begin with sincerity will not arrive at anything — and whoever does not end with sincerity will lose what he found.

school: Hanbali scholar and Persian Sufi poet of Herat (1006–1089) who combined strict adherence to the Hanbali school with ecstatic Persian mystical poetry. His Munajat (Intimate Invocations) and Manazil al-Sa’irin (Stations of the Wayfarers) are classics. Despite persecution for his theological views, he became the most beloved saint of Herat.
quotes: O God, I am not worthy of Paradise — but Paradise is not worthy of me either, for what I seek is You.
The heart that seeks other than God is like a bird that has lost its nest — it flutters everywhere and rests nowhere.
O God, I am not worthy of Paradise — but Paradise is not worthy of me either, for what I seek is You.
The heart that seeks other than God is like a bird that has lost its nest.

school: Persian Sufi scholar (c. 1009–1072) who settled in Lahore, where he is revered as ‘Data Ganj Bakhsh’ (Bestower of Treasures). His Kashf al-Mahjub is the oldest Persian treatise on Sufism and remains one of the most widely read. His shrine in Lahore is among the most visited in South Asia.

books.title: Kashf al-Mahjub (Unveiling of the Hidden)
books.note: Oldest surviving Persian prose treatise on Sufism. Full English translation free on Project Gutenberg.

quotes: The foundation of Sufism is the truth, and its pillar is sincerity.
The foundation of Sufism is the Quran and the Sunnah.
The Sufi who claims union with God while neglecting the prayer has not understood either.
Love of God is not a feeling — it is a state that transforms every action.
The foundation of Sufism is the Quran and the Sunnah.
The Sufi who claims union with God while neglecting the prayer has not understood either.
Love of God is not a feeling — it is a state that transforms every action.

school: Abu Hamid al-Ghazali (1058–1111) was a Persian theologian, jurist, philosopher, and mystic whose Ihya Ulum al-Din (Revival of the Religious Sciences) is considered the most important Islamic book after the Quran and hadith collections. He reconciled Sunni orthodoxy with Sufism, critiqued philosophy in his Tahafut al-Falasifa, and transformed Islamic intellectual life. Called ‘Hujjat al-Islam’ (Proof of Islam), he remains the most influential Muslim thinker after the Prophet.

books.title: Ihya Ulum al-Din (Revival of the Religious Sciences)
books.note: Described as 'If all Islamic books were destroyed, the Ihya alone would suffice.' 4 volumes.

books.title: Ihya Ulum al-Din (English)
books.note: Revival of the Religious Sciences — cornerstone of Islamic spirituality

books.title: Some Religious and Moral Teachings of Al-Ghazzali
Ihya Ulum al-Din (Revival of Religious Sciences)
The Incoherence of the Philosophers (Tahafut al-Falasifa)

quotes: Knowledge without action is vanity, and action without knowledge is insanity.
Declare your jihad on thirteen enemies you cannot see: egoism, arrogance, conceit, selfishness, greed, lust, intolerance, anger, lying, cheating, gossiping and slandering.
Knowledge without action is wastefulness — and action without knowledge is foolishness.

school: Persian mystic and preacher (c. 1061–1123), younger brother of the great al-Ghazali. While his brother systematised theology, Ahmad became one of the most passionate exponents of divine love mysticism. His Sawanih (Intuitions of the Lovers) is a foundational text on love in Sufi literature and influenced Rumi and the Persian poetic tradition.
quotes: Love is the essence of all spiritual states.

school: Shirazi School — Defended al-Hallaj; master of mystical Quranic commentary; wrote 5,000 visionary experiences
Khorasan School — father and first teacher of Jalal ad-Din Rumi; his Ma'arif is a mystical masterpiece

Waliullahi School — grandson of Shah Waliullah, co-founder of the Tariqa-i-Muhammadiyya with Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi; his Taqwiyat al-Iman became the founding text of Indian Islamic reformism and is still hotly debated

Tariqa-i-Muhammadiyya — student of Shah Abdul Aziz Dehlawi; led the first organised Islamic reform movement in South Asia combining Naqshbandiyya spirituality with active political resistance against Sikh rule; martyred at Balakot 1831

Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Abbas Hosseini Kashani (1931 – July 18, 2010) (Persian: السید عباس حسيني کاشانی) was an Iraqi born Iranian Twelver Shi'a Marja from Karbala whose ancestors were from Kashan, Iran hence his surname. He has studied in seminaries of Najaf, Iraq under Grand Ayatollah Abu al-Qasim al-Khoei and Muhsin al-Hakim.

Abbas Vaez Tabasi (Persian: عباس واعظ طبسی; 25 June 1935 – 4 March 2016) was an influential Iranian cleric who held memberships at different institutions. He was Grand Imam and Chairman of the Astan Quds Razavi board from 1979 until his death in 2016.
Abbas-Ali Soleimani (عباسعلی سلیمانی; 25 May 1947 – 26 April 2023) was an Iranian Shia cleric (ayatollah) and member of the Assembly of Experts. He was murdered on April 26, 2023 at age 75.

Abdulaziz ibn Abdullah Al Baz (Arabic: عبد العزيز بن عبد الله آل باز, romanized: ʿAbd al-ʿAzīz ibn ʿAbd Allāh Āl Bāz; 21 November 1912 – 13 May 1999), known as Ibn Baz or Bin Baz, was a Saudi Islamic scholar who served as the second Grand Mufti of Saudi Arabia from 1993 until his death in 1999. Ibn Baz issued a fatwa authorising a wealth tax to support the mujahideen during the anti-Soviet jihad. His endorsement of In Defence of Muslim Lands, principally written by Abdullah Azzam, was a powerful influence in the successful call for jihad against the Soviet Union. It is said to be the first official call for jihad by a nation state against another nation state in modern times.

Abdul Nabi Namazi (Persian: عبدالنبی نمازی; 1948 – 28 January 2024) was an Iranian Twelver Shia cleric and politician. He was a member of the 2nd and 3rd Assembly of Experts of the Islamic Republic of Iran, from Bushehr province. He was re-elected for the 4th and 5th Assemblies, from Tehran province and Isfahan province respectively.

Grand Ayatollah Ahmad Azari-Qomi-Bigdeli (Persian: احمد بیگدلی آذری قمی; 1925 – February 1999) was an Iranian cleric. After the 1979 Iranian Revolution he served on the Special Clerical Court and Assembly of Experts, and founded the conservative Resalat newspaper. He was arrested in November 1997 after an open letter by him was published in Britain criticizing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei for allowing torture and "moral corruption" among officials and clerics. Shortly after Khamenei denounced him in a televised speech for allegedly committing "treason against the people, the revolution and the country." His renewed candidacy for the Assembly of Experts was rejected by the Guardian Council the next year and he died in 1999.

Ahmad Ghabel (Persian: احمد قابل) was an Iranian Hojjatoleslam Shia Muslim cleric, theologian, seminary lecturer, researcher, and author. He was a follower of the dissident cleric Ayatollah Hossein Ali Montazeri and was detained several times by the Iranian government. He died on 22 October 2012 while on hospital arrest.
Grand Ayatollah Ali Asghar Rahimi Azad (Persian: علی اصغر رحیمی آزاد) (12 – 1936 August 2024) was an Iranian Twelver Shi'a Marja. He published a personal Resalah. His office is currently in Qom.
Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Ali Mohammad Dastgheib Shirazi (Persian: السيد علی‌محمد دستغیب شيرازي ) (born 14 March 1935) is an Iranian Twelver Shi'a Marja.

Ayatollah Abdul Rahman Heidari Ilami (1925 – 1 January 1987) (Persian: آیت الله عبدالرحمن حیدری ایلامی) was an Iranian Muslim scholar and member of the first term of the Assembly of Experts in Iran. He organised popular resistance to Iraq's invasion of Iran. Ayatollah Heidari was a representative of Ayatollah Khomeini in Ilam Province in the Assembly of Experts. He established himself as a conservative thinker with his comments regarding the rule of the supreme leader and statements generally against liberal ideology. Heidari died in 1986. His body was buried near the shrine of Fātimah bint Mūsā.

Bishr ibn al-Ḥārith (Arabic: بشر بن الحارث) better known as Bishr al-Ḥāfī (Bishr the Barefoot) (Arabic: بشر الحافي) was a Muslim saint born near Merv in about 767 C.E. He converted and studied Muslim tradition under Al-Fozail ibn Iyaz. Bishr became famous as one of the greatest saints in the area.

Gholam Ali Safai Bushehri (Persian: غلامعلی صفایی بوشهری) (born 22 March 1959 in Bushehr) is an Iranian Twelver Shia cleric who is the representative of Vali-e-Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist) in Bushehr Province, and the Imam of Friday Prayer in Bushehr, who has been appointed to these positions by the decree of Vali-e-Faqih, Seyyed Ali Khamenei—the supreme leader of Iran. Safai (Safayi) Bushehri was born on 22 March 1959, in the city of Bushehr in a religious family. He graduated from his elementary school in Baqeri School, and was educated at the high school of Sa'adat. This Shia cleric moved to Qom in 1983, and was educated there at Hawzah; and also taught there. Gholam Ali studied for about 17 years. Later on, he went to the school of Dar-al-Shafa', and got married at that time and has two daughters and a son. Safai Bushehri has the record of teaching at Hawzah, etc.

Haji Mirza Hassan Tabrizi (Persian: میرزا حسن تبریزی; 4 July 1851 – 12 December 1944), famously known as Haji-Mirza Hassan Roshdieh (Persian: حاجی میرزا حسن رشدیه), was an Iranian Shia cleric, teacher, politician, and journalist. He introduced some modern teaching methods in Iran, especially in teaching the alphabet. These are still used to some degree in Iran's primary schools. Hassan Roshdieh was an ethnic Iranian Azerbaijani and began studying as a Twelver Shi'a cleric there, Roshdieh abandoned his plans of going to Najaf to study in religious schools after reading an article about the hardships of education in the Persian language from the newspaper Akhtar. He left for Beirut in 1880 and studied for two years in its Daar ul-Mu'allimeen (teacher school), and then continued with visiting Istanbul and Egypt. In 1883, he left for Yerevan and founded the first modern school for Muslims there. In his new method of teaching, Roshdieh used the concept of sounds instead of alphabetic letters to teach the Persian and Azeri languages, which use the Arabic script.

Hashim Ardashir Larijani (26 February 1899 – 25 February 1993), better known as Mirza Hashim Amoli, was an Iranian Twelver Shia scholar and cleric. Born in Larijan, Iran, he is considered the patriarch of the Larijani family and is the father of Ali Larijani, the former secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council.

Hassan Mostafavi Khomeini (born 23 July 1972) is an Iranian politician and Shia cleric who has served as the custodian of the Mausoleum of Khomeini since 1995. A member of the Khomeini family, he is the grandson of the first Iranian supreme leader Ruhollah Khomeini. Hassan Khomeini has held relatively moderate and reformist views in contrast to the hardline principalist views held by his grandfather. As such, he has been at odds with the Iranian clerical establishment, especially the Guardian Council, and sidelined from mainstream politics.
Grand Ayatollah Hossein Mazaheri Isfahani (Persian: حسین مظاهری اصفهانی; born 16 November 1933) is an Iranian senior Twelver Shia Marja. He was also a member of the Third Assembly of Experts.

Kahina Bahloul (born 1979) is a French imam and Islamic academic. An adherent of Sunni Islam and Sufism, she became the first female imam in France in 2019, when she founded the unisex Fatima Mosque. She advocates liberalization and modernist reforms in Islam, including equal rights for women in Islam, the creation of a distinct French Muslim identity, and an individualist approach to religion and spirituality. Bahloul does not wear a veil, which she sees as a symbol of Islamism. She opposes traditional and conservative interpretations of the Quran and their dominance in the Muslim world. Born in Paris to an interfaith family, Bahloul grew up in Algeria where she was raised by her Muslim father. He taught her about Islam from a humanist perspective, and her faith was shaken by Islamic fundamentalism and Islamism that took hold during the Algerian Civil War. Bahloul returned to France in 2003 where she worked in insurance. The death of her father in 2009 led her to explore Sufism and become an Islamic academic. Multiple acts of Islamic terrorism in 2015 motivated her to reform and redefine Islam, leading her to found the Fatima Mosque and become an imam.

Gholamhossein Mohammadi (Persian: غلامحسین محمدی; born c. 1943), better known as Mohammad Mohammadi Golpayegani (Persian: محمد محمدی گلپایگانی), is an Iranian Shia Islamic cleric who was the Chief of Staff of the Office of the Supreme Leader of Iran from 1989 to now. He is also famous as "Hujjat al-Islam Golpayegani" was born in 1943 (1322 S.H.) in the city of Golpayegan, in Isfahan province. His father, Abul Qasim Mohammadi Golpayegani, was well known as an Islamic scholar/commentator; he was also the Imam of Friday Prayer in Golpayegan, a city northwest of Isfahan. He was among the political warriors against Pahlavi, and also among the audience in the event of attack to Feyziyeh School. Mohammadi Golpayegani is a poet, too, and there have been published poems in his name in the press of Iran. "Persian Gulf" is regarded among his prominent poems that describes it according to his words. One of his sons married Boshra Khamenei, daughter of Iran's second Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. Boshra and one of Mohammadi Golpayegani's grandchildren by the couple died during the assassination of Ali Khamenei in 2026.

Molla Vali Vidadi (Azerbaijani: Molla Vəli Vidadi) (17 March 1709, Shamkir – 13 May 1809, near Gazakh) was an Azerbaijani poet. Little is known about Vidadi. He spent most of his life in his native town of Shamkir (then called Shamkhor) where he taught at a religious school. According to some sources, Vidadi spent some years in Tiflis, Georgia, where he served as a court poet for King Erekle II. Upon the death of the king's son Levan in 1781, Vidadi wrote an elegy dedicated to the deceased. His court service did not last long, possibly due to his open criticism of the ruling class. At its early stage, Vidadi's poetry expressed a positive view on life, however as years passed, his poems began expressing a more pessimistic attitude. His was also known for his poetic dialogue with Molla Panah Vagif. Vidadi's simple and realistic (sometimes involving elements of mysticism) literary style resembled the traditional Azeri ashik genre.

Muḥammad Ṣāliḥ al-Munajjid (Arabic: محمد صالح المنجد; born 14 June 1961 (30 Dhu al-Hijjah 1380 AH)) is a Syrian-born Palestinian-Saudi Islamic scholar. He is the founder of the fatwa website IslamQA, the most popular website for responses on the topic of Islam.

Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Alawi ibn al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Aziz (1944–2004), also known as Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki, (Arabic: محمد بن علوي المالكي) was one of the foremost traditional Sunni Islamic scholar of contemporary times from Saudi Arabia. He is considered to be the Mujaddid of 20th-21st century.

Muqtada al-Sadr (born 4 August 1974) is an Iraqi cleric, politician and former militia leader. A leading Shia Muslim, he inherited the leadership of the Sadrist Movement from his father, and founded the now-dissolved Mahdi Army militia in 2003 that resisted the American occupation of Iraq. He also founded the Promised Day Brigade insurgent group after the dissolution of the Mahdi Army; both were backed by Iran. In 2014, he founded the Peace Companies militia and serves as its current head. In 2018, he joined his Sadrist political party to the Saairun alliance, which won the highest number of seats in the 2018 and 2021 Iraqi parliamentary elections.

Ayatollah Seyed Reza Zanjani (born 1902 in Zanjan, died 4 January 1984) was a Shia Iranian cleric who opposed first the autocracy of Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and then theocracy that was established by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini and his supporters following the Islamic revolution. Reza Zanjani was an Azeri from Zanjan province in Northwest Iran. He was active in politics, supporting Prime Minister Mohammed Mosaddeq, and following Mossaddeq's overthrow in the 1953 coup invited nationalist and religious opposition groups to join the National Resistance Movement. In the years before the 1979 Islamic revolution he is said to have "retained close contacts with the secular National Front as well as the religious-minded Freedom Movement of Iran." Along with Ayatollahs Golpayegani, Helani, and Mohammad Kazem Shariatmadari, he supported Constitutional monarchy for Iran, including the 1906 Constitution's original provisions for committee of Shi'a clerics to approve all legislation. When the Islamic revolution established a constitution with a Shia jurist as supreme leader, Zanjani opposed it.

Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Ali Asghar Dastgheib (Persian: سید علی‌اصغر دستغیب) (born 1945) is an Iranian Twelver Shia Marja and former member of the Iranian Assembly of Experts. He was among those who advised Ruhollah Khomeini in probate matters.

Sidi Boushaki or Ibrahim Ibn Faïd Ez-Zaouaoui (Arabic: إبراهيم بن فايد الزواوي) (1394 CE/796 AH – 1453 CE/857 AH) was a Maliki theologian born near the town of Thenia, 54 km (34 mi) east of Algiers. He was raised in a very spiritual environment with high Islamic values and ethics within the Algerian Islamic reference.

Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Taqi Tabatabaei Qomi (Arabic: تقي الطباطبايي القمي Persian: تقى طباطبايى قمى, February 21, 1923–October 26, 2016) was an Iranian Shia marja'. He, along with Ali al-Sistani, and Ali Falsafi, were among the only three people to receive a written statement declaring their ijtihad by grand Ayatollah Abu al-Qasim al-Khoei.

Sheikh Yasser al-Habib (Arabic: ياسر الحبيب; born 20 January 1979) is a Kuwaiti Shia scholar, and the head of the London-based Mahdi Servants Union, as well as Al-Muhassin mosque in Fulmer, Buckinghamshire, and the writer of The Lady of Heaven. Al-Habib's work focuses on Islamic history, drawing on Shia and Sunni sources. Al-Habib started his religious activities in Kuwait, starting off as a member of the Dawah Party, later he founded a non-profit religious organization named Khoddam Al-Mahdi Organization, and he also expressed his religious views regarding Abu Bakr and Umar, and criticized them sharply. This resulted in anger from several Sunni speakers in Kuwait, such as Othman al-Khamees, and other Arabic-speaking Sunni communities, which finally led to the arrest of al-Habib. Later, in February 2004 he was released under an annual pardon announced by the Emir of Kuwait on the occasion of the country's National Day, but his rearrest was ordered a few days later. Al-Habib fled Kuwait before he was sentenced in absentia to 10 years imprisonment, and spent months in Iraq and Iran before gaining asylum in the United Kingdom.

Grand Ayatollah Sayyid Yousef Madani Tabrizi (Persian: السيد يوسف مدنی تبریزی; 1928-16 June 2013) was an Iranian Twelver Shi'a Marja. He studied in seminaries of Qum, Iran under Grand Ayatollah Seyyed Hossein Borujerdi and Muhammad Husayn Tabatabaei. He is the author of many Islamic books. He died due to old age on 16 June 2013.

Abu Sa'id Abu'l-Khayr was one of the earliest and most influential Persian Sufi masters. He is credited with pioneering the mystical quatrain (ruba'i) form later perfected by Omar Khayyam and establishing one of the first formal Sufi communities with written rules of conduct. His ecstatic utterances and antinomian behaviour made him a legendary figure in Persian mystical literature.

Beket Ata was a revered Kazakh Sufi saint, scholar, and healer from the Mangystau Peninsula. He established several underground mosques carved into the rock of the Ustyurt Plateau, the most famous at Oglandy. His shrine remains the most visited pilgrimage site in western Kazakhstan and he is venerated as a protector and spiritual guide by the Kazakh people.
